attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic with three months of progressive abdominal distension and early satiety. She reports vague lower abdominal discomfort and has lost 5 kg in weight. She experienced menopause at age 52. She has a medical history of hypertension and takes amlodipine. She has never smoked.

On examination, she appears tired. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her abdomen is distended with shifting dullness. There is a firm, irregular mass palpable in the left lower quadrant. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals a complex 8 cm left adnexal mass with solid and cystic components.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following combinations of tumour markers is most appropriate to request?
options: Carcinoembryonic antigen and human chorionic gonadotropin
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suspicious for ovarian cancer, including progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, weight loss, and ascites. The ultrasound findings of a complex adnexal mass further support this diagnosis. In investigating suspected ovarian cancer, appropriate tumour marker selection is crucial.

CA125, carcinoembryonic antigen, and CA19.9 is the most appropriate combination. CA125 is elevated in about 80% of epithelial ovarian cancers but can also be elevated in other conditions including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and other malignancies. CEA and CA19.9 can also be elevated in primary ovarian cancer or can also indicate gastrointestinal malignancies that may have metastasised to the ovary.

Carcinoembryonic antigen and human chorionic gonadotropin would miss CA125, which is the most important marker in epithelial ovarian cancer. Human chorionic gonadotropin is primarily used for gestational trophoblastic disease and germ cell tumours.

Alpha-fetoprotein and human chorionic gonadotropin are typically used to investigate germ cell tumours, which are rare in postmenopausal women. These tumours typically present in younger patients and often have different clinical features.

CA125 and alpha-fetoprotein Alpha-fetoprotein is more useful in younger patients with suspected germ cell tumours.

CA125 and inhibin would be more appropriate for investigating granulosa cell tumours, which are uncommon in this age group and typically present with different clinical features.
learningPoints: CA125 is the most sensitive tumour marker for epithelial ovarian cancer but has poor specificity.
Multiple tumour markers (CA125, CEA, CA19.9) should be measured in suspected ovarian cancer
Normal tumour markers do not exclude ovarian cancer.

Women <40 years old should also have testing for BHCG, AFP and LDH which can be elevated in germ cell tumours. Germ cell tumours only occur in younger pre-menopausal women so these tumour markers are not required for postmenopausal patients.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). Ovarian Cysts in Postmenopausal Women: Green-top Guideline No. 34 [Internet]. London: RCOG; 2016. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/ovarian-cysts-in-postmenopausal-women-green-top-guideline-no-34/

Chew D, Solomon D. Investigation and management of an ovarian mass [Internet]. Aust Fam Physician. 2015 [cited 2024 Nov 29];44(1-2):38-42. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2015/january-february/investigation-and-management-of-an-ovarian-mass
